data.title: If You Believe In Transformative AI Healthcare Then These Three Stocks Could Define Your Next Cycle
data.description:
Artificial intelligence in healthcare sits at the crossroads of rising inflation pressures, higher bond yields, and ongoing geopolitical tension, as investors search for themes tied to real-world problem solving and efficiency. AI tools that help doctors detect disease, personalise treatment, and reduce hospital bottlenecks speak directly to cost control and better patient outcomes, two priorities that tend to matter regardless of the growth or rate cycle. This article highlights three stocks from a Transformative AI Healthcare screener, providing a closer look at companies using data and algorithms to address diagnostics, treatment, and care delivery at scale.

Advertisement

GeneDx Holdings (WGS)

Overview: GeneDx Holdings is a genomics company that provides whole exome and whole genome sequencing for pediatric and rare diseases, using AI to analyze DNA and deliver clinical-grade test reports for rare disease, hereditary risk, and cancer. It also sells data and information services built on its large rare disease dataset.

Operations: GeneDx generates almost all of its US$427.5 million revenue in the United States, with US$423.1 million from its core Gene Dx segment and US$4.5 million from Fabric Genomics and legacy Sema4.

Market Cap: US$2.0b

GeneDx sits at the center of AI driven genomics in pediatrics, with whole exome and genome sequencing supported by a rare disease database and an AI platform that aims to make diagnosis faster and more precise. Analysts expect strong revenue and earnings growth. The stock currently trades well below some fair value estimates and price targets, which may appeal to investors who focus on mispriced growth. At the same time, the company is unprofitable, relies on external funding, and is seeing insider selling, so execution on reimbursement, volume growth, and cost discipline really matters. Recent deals, such as the Zevra NPC testing program and a new term loan, illustrate how GeneDx is trying to scale its model and strengthen its finances.

AI fueled genomics, a rare disease dataset and a stock trading below some fair value estimates can create an intriguing mismatch. See how the 3 key rewards and 1 important warning sign could reshape your view of the trade off hiding in GeneDx.

Pfizer (PFE)

Overview: Pfizer is a global pharmaceutical company that discovers, develops, manufactures, and sells prescription medicines and vaccines for conditions such as cardiovascular disease, migraine, cancer, rare diseases, infections, inflammation, and COVID 19, supported by partnerships with companies like Bristol Myers Squibb, Astellas, Merck KGaA, BioNTech, and others.

Operations: Pfizer generates the bulk of its US$62.2b revenue from its Global Biopharmaceuticals Business at US$61.2b, with smaller contributions from Pfizer Centreone at US$1.3b and Pfizer Ignite at US$41m, and earns US$37.1b in the United States versus US$25.5b across developed and emerging markets.

Market Cap: US$153.6b

Pfizer gives you exposure to artificial intelligence in healthcare through its work on biomolecular AI models, while still being anchored by a broad portfolio of medicines and vaccines in areas like oncology, rare disease, and metabolic health. The pipeline is busy, from obesity and GLP 1 programs and oncology data at ASCO 2026 to a Lyme disease vaccine candidate and priority reviews for cancer combinations. At the same time, the company is also dealing with earnings and revenue expected to decline over the next few years and questions around dividend coverage and debt. For investors weighing that mix of AI enabled R&D, late stage assets, pricing pressure, and patent risk, the full story goes well beyond headline P/E ratios and a high dividend yield.

Pfizer’s AI driven R&D and broad drug portfolio could be masking a very different risk reward profile than headline earnings suggest. Review the full 1 key reward and 4 important warning signs (3 are major!)

Insmed (INSM)

Overview: Insmed develops and commercializes therapies for serious and rare diseases, anchored by ARIKAYCE for difficult lung infections and a late stage pipeline that targets bronchiectasis, pulmonary hypertension, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and other hard to treat conditions across the United States, Europe, Japan, and additional regions.

Operations: Insmed generates US$606.4 million in revenue from developing and commercializing therapies for rare disease patients, with about US$453.0 million from the United States and US$153.5 million from international markets.

Market Cap: US$29.2b

Insmed sits at the intersection of AI driven protein engineering and late stage respiratory drugs. Brensocatib and ARIKAYCE label expansion are viewed as key drivers, alongside broader work in gene therapy and pulmonary hypertension. Analysts project revenue and earnings growth, yet the company is still loss making, carries a very high P/S multiple, and relies on external funding and ongoing share issuance, so execution on brensocatib launches, payer access, and Phase II and III readouts remains important. For investors who can tolerate clinical, regulatory, and dilution risk, the combination of rare disease focus, management continuity, and ambitious guidance on future respiratory revenues may make this a name to watch more closely as AI enabled drug development in complex lung conditions progresses.

Insmed’s AI driven respiratory push, rare disease focus, and high P/S valuation could be telling a very different story than the headlines suggest, and the analyst forecasts for Insmed may reveal the twist investors are missing

data.title: Assessing Vulcan Energy Resources (ASX:VUL) Valuation After German Royalty Exemption For Lionheart Project

On a simple yardstick, Vulcan Energy Resources trades on a P/B of 1.2x, which sits well below both its peer group at 5.2x and the wider Australian Metals and Mining industry at 2x.

P/B compares the market value of the equity to the accounting value of net assets, so it can be a useful check for asset heavy businesses such as miners and resource developers. A P/B of 1.2x suggests the market is not paying a large premium over the book value of Vulcan’s assets, even though the company remains loss making and is funded entirely by higher risk external borrowing rather than customer deposits.

Relative to both direct peers and the broader industry, the gap is clear. The P/B multiple is substantially lower than the 5.2x peer average and also meaningfully below the 2x sector average. This points to the market assigning a more cautious valuation to Vulcan compared to other Metals and Mining names on this measure.
